# Questions in Livestream 128

Live Stream #128: Knowledge Gaps Filled Fast

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=333" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:33</a> | When did El become a generic word for God, and was it already generic when Yahweh was called El? | He says El was already a generic word for God long before that period. | He says the word was generic well before the patriarchal period, pointing to Akkadian ilu in the Old Akkadian period around 2500 BC. In his explanation, Mesopotamian gods had specific names such as Marduk, Shamash, and Ishtar, while El or ilu could function more generally as a word for deity before later proper-name usage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=436" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:16</a> | Why do many very early Exodus daters place the Exodus before 1500 BC or with a pharaoh whose real dates are before 1500 BC? | He says many alternative Exodus theories depend on chronological revisionism. | He argues that very early Exodus theories often revise chronology to make preferred theories fit the data, rather than adjusting theories to the evidence. He gives examples such as Gerald Aardsma placing the Exodus near the pyramid age, David Rohl moving the Middle Bronze and Middle Kingdom, and early-date advocates adjusting Egyptian reigns to fit 1446 BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=729" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:09</a> | If only scribes and priests were allowed in Egyptian temples, what about the pharaoh? | The king is the high priest of every Egyptian cult and was allowed in temple contexts. | He says the king was not an exception barred from temples, but the ultimate authorized priest. In Egyptian ideology the king had the right and duty to enter temples, perform the highest rituals for every cult, and maintain the religious system, so temple access by the pharaoh was built into the system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=852" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:12</a> | Who does he prefer, John Walton or Kenneth Kitchen? | He chooses Kitchen, while saying he also likes Walton and is biased because of his relationship with Kitchen. | He says Kitchen has a special place for him because he spent three years as Kitchen's research assistant and learned things from him that he would not have learned in classes. He still speaks positively of John Walton and his writing, but openly frames the answer as biased by personal debt and affection toward Kitchen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:20</a> | Was there a minimum age to be an heir designate? | No. The decisive factor was being the firstborn, usually the firstborn male. | He says age was not the determining issue; status as the firstborn was. Usually that meant the oldest male child, though he allows that an oldest daughter could be heir designate in a family with only daughters, and he notes that the heir could theoretically be a newborn. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=1088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:08</a> | Could Egyptians have painted blood on their doorframes, or was Passover only available to Israelites? | He works from Exodus 12 and treats the warning and ritual as directed to Israel. | After reading Exodus 12, he distinguishes the recipients of the warning from the effect of the sign. The instructions were given to Israel, but he says an Egyptian who heard from an Israelite neighbor and performed the Passover ritual would also have been passed over, while an Israelite who ignored the ritual would have faced the plague. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=1357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:37</a> | Did he see InspiringPhilosophy's debate with Eric Hovind, and what were his thoughts? | Yes. His reaction was sharply negative toward Hovind's performance. | He criticizes Hovind for inconsistency, for claiming the age-of-the-earth issue is not salvific while treating it as foundational, and for accusing others of worshiping a different God. He also says Hovind imported ideas into Genesis, mishandled later revelation, relied on the King James Version, and lacked the Hebrew needed for the argument he was making. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=2164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:04</a> | What does he think of Seth Fleishman's suggestion that Amalek is the Lukka people? | He thinks the proposal has some plausibility. | He treats the Lukka-Amalek proposal as possible rather than certain. His answer allows some plausibility because groups in the region and period can be hard to pin down, but he does not present the identification as established. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=2229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:09</a> | What does he make of ley lines and ancient holy buildings aligned to mystical energy lines? | He rejects ley lines as modern nonsense and does not think ancient builders used them that way. | He says apparent alignments can arise by chance once enough buildings are plotted, but that there is no evidence for mystical energy lines. He identifies ley-line ideas with the twentieth-century Earth Mysteries movement and calls them pseudoscientific rather than ancient. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=2373" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:33</a> | How do we know Saul of Tarsus was from Tarsus in Turkey rather than Tartessos in Spain? | He distinguishes Tarsus from Tartessos and says the evidence points to Tarsus in Cilicia. | He corrects the geography by distinguishing Tarsus in Cilicia from Tarshish or Tartessos, often associated with Spain or sometimes Sardinia. Tarsus is a known city in southeastern Turkey with earlier Hittite and Neo-Assyrian attestations, and he says Paul is from that Tarsus, while Tarshish is a separate debated location. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=2614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:34</a> | If Romans 5:12 says death is the result of sin, does infant death mean babies go to hell? | No. He argues that grace is extended and that children are not condemned for parental sin. | He warns against proof-texting Romans 5:12 and reads the wider passage through verse 17 as an arc from Adamic death to Christ's greater gift. He argues that under the new covenant grace is extended, children are not culpable for their parents' sin, and babies do not sin in Adam's likeness of rebellion, so they are not destined for hell. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=3284" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:44</a> | Does the archaeological record indicate that Gibeon was uninhabited during the time of Joshua? | He says Gibeon's record is complicated, but he thinks there was Late Bronze occupation. | He compares Gibeon to Jericho: Early Bronze and Middle Bronze occupation, an apparent Late Bronze blank in the city remains, then Iron Age occupation, while Late Bronze tombs still exist. He suggests Late Bronze occupation may have been leveled for later construction, so the absence of a surviving Late Bronze city layer does not mean no Late Bronze presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=3509" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:29</a> | Would he recommend Robert Alter's Art of Biblical Narrative, translation, and Hebrew Bible commentary? | He had not read the translation or Art of Biblical Narrative, so he keeps the recommendation limited. | He says he has recommended Alter's Art of Biblical Poetry, but had not read the translation, the commentary, or Art of Biblical Narrative. Because of that, he does not give a firm recommendation beyond saying Alter is a good author and that he would probably read the work if he had the occasion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=3679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:19</a> | How did people in the Late Bronze Age view good and evil? | He says the categories were somewhat different and tied to ancient cultural and religious assumptions. | He says Late Bronze views were less like modern objective moral categories and more tied to what was good or bad for a person in relation to divine will or divine action. In Egypt, gods could have beneficent and dangerous aspects; in Canaanite settings, goodness could be framed by the will of a deity such as El, with power and divine favor shaping moral evaluation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=4063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:43</a> | Do Levantine religions have something parallel to the Ark of the Covenant, apart from Egyptian religion? | Not in the same way; he says there are parallels to cherubim, but Egyptian influence is more relevant. | He says there is no clear Levantine parallel to the Ark itself, although winged throne imagery and cherubim-like figures are known and may reflect Egyptian iconographic influence. He adds that preservation of Levantine texts and furniture is limited, so the cautious answer is that there is no evidence, not that such an object could never have existed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=4183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:43</a> | If Israelite DNA is indistinguishable from Bronze Age Canaanite DNA, why should Israelites not have at least some Egyptian DNA? | He says DNA evidence does not require that conclusion and must be interpreted cautiously. | He says Israelites in the Delta would have been among Semitic Asiatic populations, so their DNA would not necessarily be sharply different from Bronze Age Canaanites. He also notes Egyptian aversion to marrying or even eating with Semitic Asiatics, making extensive Egyptian intermarriage less likely, while later intermarriage with Canaanites would further explain Canaanite-like DNA. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=4402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:22</a> | How much chronological data does the Groundhog program have, and does it include New Testament anchor points? | It does not include the New Testament period; it was built around earlier chronological data. | He says Groundhog does not include New Testament chronology because its cutoff is around the sixth century BC and earlier ancient Near Eastern chronology. He gives approximate contents of about 500 kings and 200 synchronisms, says New Testament chronology is already comparatively well established, and notes that Greek chronology might be added someday. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=4884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:24</a> | How does someone get involved in chronology? | He jokes that one has to be born into it, then stresses that chronology is extremely difficult. | He jokes darkly about needing to be stubborn, but the substantive answer is that chronology requires comfort with ambiguity, attention to detail, ancient Near Eastern breadth, and often computer skills. He stresses that the field has many open questions and rewards, but also many risks and cranks, so a person should only enter it if genuinely interested. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=5502" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:42</a> | What is the genre of Jonah, and was Jonah really eaten? | He says Jonah mixes genres and should not be reduced to only one simple category. | He says Jonah includes mixed genres, including poetic and narrative elements, and he personally believes Jonah was really swallowed. He considers the swallowing itself less difficult than Jonah's death and restoration, and connects the fish to Jesus' sign of Jonah, where the fish functions typologically like a tomb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=5757" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:57</a> | Given shakeups in higher education, where are specialist fields like Egyptology and archaeology going? | He expects departments may become fewer and more consolidated, but the fields will not simply disappear. | He says the humanities have been losing ground for decades as resources shift toward STEM fields, producing fewer specialist departments and less reading-intensive training. His expectation is consolidation: independent departments may merge, enrollment may shrink, and roles like Egyptology may be phased out in many places, though a few programs will remain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=5880" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:00</a> | Is archaeology a STEM field? | No. He says archaeology is still classed as a humanity or art, not a STEM field. | He answers directly that archaeology is not STEM, defining STEM as science, technology, engineering, and medicine. He places archaeology in the humanities or arts, even while recognizing that modern academic funding pressures often favor STEM over humanities disciplines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=6566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:26</a> | What is his opinion on Adam and Abraham's historicity? | He thinks both Adam and Abraham were historical figures. | He says Abraham was a real historical figure and mentions having made videos on synchronisms involving Abraham and figures such as Hammurabi. He also thinks Adam was historical, while describing the Adam story as shaped through a reception-history and temple-inauguration lens rather than as bare modern historiography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=6656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:56</a> | Any thoughts on the new Oxyrhynchus sayings fragment and possible use of Luke, Matthew, or Thomas? | He had not seen the latest text and therefore answers only cautiously. | He says he recognizes the Oxyrhynchus context but had not read the latest fragment, so his comments are tentative. He notes that sayings texts circulated, that Oxyrhynchus has yielded gospel material before, and that possible Thomas material would point toward later or Gnostic-adjacent material, but he does not think the fragment would significantly affect gospel historicity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=6803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:23</a> | Regarding creation ex nihilo, did the ancients think God made the waters too, or were they preexistent? | He treats ex nihilo as meaning God created even the waters, not merely brought order to preexistent stuff. | He explains ex nihilo as creation out of absence, while noting that some ancient myths, including Mesopotamian and Egyptian examples, treated primordial waters as eternal. He says the Bible points more toward absolute nothingness at some point, with the waters created and then separated, though he distinguishes Genesis 1's creation-space focus from later New Testament ex nihilo formulation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=7098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:18</a> | Are Jesus and John the Baptist apocalyptic Jews, and does that imply they denied a soul separate from the body? | He says they believed in a soul and does not accept that implication. | He says Jesus clearly believed in a soul and life after death, while the evidence for John is thinner. Jesus may be called apocalyptic in a qualified sense, but not in the same way as some first-century apocalyptic groups, and Jesus' appeal to God as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob supports ongoing life beyond bodily death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=7402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:22</a> | How did ancient Egyptian music work, did they notate it, and what instruments are shown? | He says we do not know exactly how the music worked, though instruments and scale theories can be discussed. | He says Egyptian music may have used a five-note Egyptian scale, but the evidence for how early that scale reaches is uncertain. He knows of no Egyptian music notation texts, though notation existed elsewhere, and he lists instruments such as flute, sistrum or rattle, lyres or harps, clappers, and tomb-preserved or painted examples used for religious and entertainment contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=7628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:08</a> | What is his general view of how inspiration works, especially with Septuagint and Masoretic readings? | He applies his normal doctrine of inspiration while allowing textual criticism to compare manuscript traditions. | He defines inspiration as God providentially preparing a writer through life experience, education, failures, family, work, and reading, then using that preparation as the writer chooses words for the inspired text. For Septuagint and Masoretic differences, he says textual criticism must compare traditions such as the Septuagint, Masoretic Text, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Dead Sea Scrolls to approach the autographs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=8437" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:37</a> | Does he plan to review the final excavation report from Khirbet el-Maqatir when the volume on biblical Ai appears? | He had not planned on it and gives reasons for caution about committing to that review. | He says he had not planned to review it and does not think Khirbet el-Maqatir is biblical Ai. His caution comes from distrust of Associates for Biblical Research's analysis: he praises their field archaeology but says they can omit disliked data, redate typologies, or twist interpretation to support an early Exodus framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=9159" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:39</a> | Why are flood stories so widespread if there is no scientific evidence for a global flood? | He says flood stories are widespread, but that does not by itself establish the specific global-flood claim. | He acknowledges that flood stories are widespread and often involve divine anger or starting over, but shifts to the theological function of the Genesis flood. He describes Noah's ark as a sacralizing converter: a box that carries profane life from the old world through a water purification ritual so that people and animals emerge made appropriate for the renewed world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=9621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:21</a> | Has he read Mika Waltari's The Egyptian? | No. He says he has not read it and prefers working from the original texts. | He says he has not read Waltari's novel and prefers original texts, then discusses the Egyptian tale of Sinuhe. He recommends reading Sinuhe, says William Kelly Simpson's translation is good, and notes that Sinuhe may resemble a tomb biography more than pure fiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=10519" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:19</a> | If Hyksos means foreign rulers, why would Hyksos kings use it as a title in cartouches and scarabs? | He says there was a tribal or group affiliation behind the term, so the title was not simply an insult. | He says the title distinguished the Fifteenth Dynasty rulers from the Semitic warlords of the Fourteenth Dynasty, and that an originally Egyptian pejorative could be adopted and owned by the group it described. In his framing, Hyksos rulers used the title to signal dominance: they were foreigners, but foreigners ruling Egypt and collecting its taxes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=10759" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:19</a> | What is his opinion of Michael Homan's To Your Tents, O Israel and views on the history of the Tabernacle? | He says there is a range of views, but he had not read Homan's book and keeps the answer qualified. | He says he has not read Homan's book, then sketches the range of views: a majority view that the tabernacle was mythical, a minority view that it was built in the Sinai covenant context, and several middle positions that see later shaping or reduced grandeur. He adds that excavated Shiloh makes it hard for him to accept that there was no tabernacle at all. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=11017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:37</a> | Is it irredeemably heretical to believe the waters were always there? | He says yes, while allowing the questioner to explain a model elsewhere. | He says he thinks eternal waters are heretical and also inconsistent with modern cosmology, because the universe and earth had a beginning. Biblically, he says prophetic imagery of the heavens being stretched out points away from eternal matter, while he still allows the questioner to explain the proposed model in Discord. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=11425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:25</a> | Does God ever lead someone to make art? | Yes. He says God can lead someone to make art and illustrates from his own experience. | He says God can lead a person to make art, drawing on his own background in a family of artists and an example of a painting about abortion that opened a serious conversation. He also criticizes narrow church assumptions that only music or drama count as art, arguing that visual art and other forms can also be legitimate callings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=13338" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:18</a> | If Adam and Cain were farmers and Tubal-Cain worked bronze and iron, would that put early pre-flood times in the Bronze or Iron Age? | He says those labels cannot be mapped simply onto Genesis in that way. | He says Genesis is using retrospection rather than giving a modern archaeological period label for pre-flood history. Cain and Abel contrast sedentary agriculture and pastoralism, while Tubal-Cain's bronze and iron language reflects the audience's later knowledge and the writer's way of connecting origins to practices known in his own time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=13536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:36</a> | Could God have wanted people not to remember the sin of Noah's generation and left no flood evidence except the rainbow? | He says it is a difficult question and treats the premise cautiously. | He says the answer depends heavily on whether one reads the flood as local or global and how one interprets Genesis 5 and 11 genealogies. A local-flood view might not predict global evidence, while young-earth or other approaches define evidence differently, so what counts as evidence depends on the interpretive framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=14616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:36</a> | Could the destruction of Hazor level 16 be contemporary with the Middle Bronze Jericho IV destruction? | No. He says those destructions are separated by about 200 years. | He says Hazor level 16 belongs to Middle Bronze II, while Jericho City IV's destruction belongs to Middle Bronze III. Because those phases are separated by roughly two centuries, he rejects making them the same destruction event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=14670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:30</a> | How can Deuteronomy's fortified-city language fit the thirteenth century BC rather than the Middle Bronze period? | He says the language is hyperbolic and that Late Bronze casemate walls fit the biblical descriptions better. | He reads Deuteronomy's cities fortified to heaven as hyperbole, not a literal claim that every city had massive Middle Bronze walls. He says many Late Bronze towns were hamlets or had mudbrick casemate walls, and Rahab living in Jericho's wall fits a casemate-wall setting better than thick Middle Bronze stone fortifications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=14955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:15</a> | Are the Dead Sea Scrolls authentic? | Most are, especially excavated and museum-held scrolls; he warns about forgeries on the antiquities market. | He says the vast majority are authentic, especially scrolls properly excavated and held in museums. His warning is about fragments from the private antiquities market, where forgeries have appeared and have even caused scholars' work to be retracted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SCiHmB9L850?t=15038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:38</a> | Did archaeologists find Jericho's wall fallen outward? | He says that claim comes from interpretation of earlier excavations and that the issue is more complicated. | He says the Bible itself does not state that Jericho's wall fell outward. The claim comes from early excavation interpretation of Middle Bronze wall collapse and ramp-like debris, but he says more recent interpretation sees earthquake collapse followed by grading or smoothing by an invader, so readers should not go beyond the biblical text without warrant. |
